data.title: Clorox: Reduced Complexity Paves The Way For Multiple Expansion
data.description:
Clorox (CLX) has been underperforming on a YTD basis, and in the last year alone, it is down nearly 29%. This poor performance somewhat misses the mark on improving fundamentals and there could be an opportunity to own the shares here, post-FQ3 earnings. With a 5% dividend yield, the stock screens rather attractively.

Business Profile

Clorox makes branded cleaning and household products under brands like its namesake Clorox, Pine-Sol, and Glad. The company is also the owner of a wide variety of other brands like Kingsford, best known for charcoal grilling; Burt's Bees, best known for lip and skin care; and Hidden Valley Ranch. The company operates in nearly 100+ countries around the world and has a leading position in at least 80% of the categories it operates in.

Clorox has four key segments - Health & Wellness, Household, Lifestyle, and International. By revenue mix, Health & Wellness is approximately 38%, Household is 28%, Lifestyle is 18%, and International is 15%.

Within this, it's worth noting that Clorox actually gives us a bit more detail on the product mixes that they sell. The company has disclosed that cleaning products are 44% of the business, bags and wraps are 15%, food products are 12%, and cat litter is 10%.

It's worth noting the customer concentration for Clorox. The company has Walmart (WMT) as its largest customer at 27% of sales. No other customer accounts for more than 10% of sales, but the five largest customers for the company account for nearly half of the company's net sales in the last three fiscal years. So, it is very heavily dependent on a few select retailers globally. The company is largely domestic with only a small international exposure, approximately 85% domestic.

The company also just recently purchased GOJO Industries, which makes Purell. The company paid $2.25 billion in cash. This included certain tax benefits of $330 million, so the net purchase price was $1.92 billion. Purell does $800 million in annual sales and has done 5% sales growth over the last 5 years. Clorox has a long-term algorithm for 3-5% sales growth, so this is accretive to the top line. The company noted in the deal closure press release that it will be accretive to EBITDA and EPS in the second year, not the first year. Synergies will total approximately $50 million.

This is the company's first major acquisition in several years, with most other brands in the portfolio having been acquired pre-COVID.

Source: Investor Presentation

Clorox is in an interesting vertical within consumer staples because, for the tracked channels it operates in, the company has seen private label competition share stagnate at around 17%, rather than increase. This is a much more favorable profile than other verticals.

Source: Investor Presentation

Financial Profile & Fiscal Q3 Earnings Setup

With the recent M&A news, the transaction is largely an FY27 consideration from a full earnings standpoint. With that being said, it's worth considering the state of the balance sheet. As of last quarter, the company had $2.56 billion in net debt ($227 million in cash against total debt of $2.79 billion).

If we consider the adjusted operating income profile in FY26 to be approximately $1.0 billion (which assumes they hit the midpoint of guidance), then leverage on a net debt/adjusted EBITDA basis is approximately 2.1x. After the financing for Gojo begins, net debt will increase to about $4.5 billion, and that assumes the tax benefits have reduced the purchase price like the acquisition release mentions. Then, we have to consider that the transaction adds about $160 million in EBITDA (given the purchase price at 11.9x) and look out to fiscal '27 when it will be formally included in total Clorox operations. Adj. EBITDA then will be just shy of $1.6 billion, and net leverage will be 2.8x.

So, it's not the most comfortable balance sheet in consumer staples, but there are two important things to consider. First, Purell is a high quality brand with high awareness and a category leading position. Second, the company produces enough free cash flow in a year (approximately ~$770 million in FY25 alone) where they can delever close to three quarters of a turn, if not a full turn post-Gojo, to where the balance sheet can be in check under 2x net leverage by FY28. This staves away leverage concerns.

With that being said, the company is coming off of a great FY25 where they had 7% organic volume growth and 5% organic sales growth. This was largely driven by incremental shipments that happened following a recovery from both their cyberattack and ERP transition, as there were shelf space disruptions as a result of these factors. The ERP tailwind was worth about 3.5-4pts of the 5% OSG% growth.

As we approach Q3 earnings this week, the setup seems relatively straight forward.

Recall the guide:

  • Net sales -6% to -10%
    • <1 point impact from VMS divestiture
    • OSG% -5% to -9%
    • 7.5% impact from reversal of the impact of incremental shipments associated with the prior year
  • GM% down 50-100 bps
  • SG&A 16% of sales
    • 90 bps of impact from strategic investments
  • Advertising 11% of sales
  • Tax rate 24%
  • Adj. EPS $5.95-$6.30 (down 18-23% y/y)
    • Excludes 35 cents from strategic investments; includes 90 cents from reversal of the impact from incremental shipments associated with ERP

The company is lapping a net sales figure of -8% y/y and an organic sales figure of -2% y/y.

The -8% bridge to -2% is primarily due to the VMS and Argentina business divestiture, so this doesn't need to be reconciled. The -2% organic sales figure was primarily driven by unfavorable price mix, rather than one-time factors like ERP.

I am modeling organic sales growth of 2% but reported growth of 1%, gross margin contraction of 50 bps, and SG&A at 16% flat with an 11% advertising rate, normal below the line items, including a 24% tax rate. This equates to $1.65 in adj. EPS vs. street at $1.54. This should set up a positive reaction for shares.

Valuation & FY27 Estimates

What we have here with CLX is a normalization. The cyber attack and the ERP change are moving behind the company after FY26, as is the incremental shelf restocking. This is setting a clean base of financials for next year, so I don't believe it's appropriate to value CLX on a fiscal 2026 basis.

The simplest way to understand the earnings potential for next year on CLX is to assume that they hit the midpoint of their Adj. EPS guidance this year, which would equate to $6.13.

Recall that the company has a financial algo in place:

  • Sales growth of 3-5%
  • Adj. EBIT Margin expansion of +25-50bps
  • FCF of 11-13%

If we assume FY27 is on-algo, then we get $7.4 billion in total sales and adjusted operating income of $1.2 billion.

After factoring in interest expense of about $90 million and a tax rate of 24%, we arrive at a net income of $6.79. This would be a growth of approximately 10.8%, assuming the midpoint of guidance this year is hit. I am also assuming that the other income/expense line item before pre-tax earnings is negligible. There is no clear pattern for forecasting it.

Taking a step back, that would put CLX on an FY27 EPS of 14.5x. That is relatively cheap for double-digit earnings growth and 4% top line growth. There would even be scope for multiple expansions to the 17-18x area. That would be a $120 stock for 20% upside from the current level. This also assumes no accretion from Gojo next year.

Additionally, the dividend yield of 5% is certainly starting to pop up on screeners and can add to the total return profile. There are not many large-cap consumer staples names with a 5% yield.

Versus peers, valuation is as follows:

  • Procter & Gamble (PG) - trades at 21.6x forward earnings
  • Kimberly-Clark (KMB) - trades at 13.8x forward earnings
  • Church & Dwight (CHD) - trades at 25.4x forward earnings
  • Colgate (CL) - trades at 22.1x forward earnings

Clorox is getting more of a Kimberly-Clark multiple, despite about ~2 points better top line growth on a forward basis and while the earnings growth rate is similar in the low-double-digit region, investors don't have to stomach a major acquisition like that of Kenvue (KVUE). There is clear room for the Clorox multiple to expand.

Source: Passage Research

Risks

Investing with Clorox comes with several risks. First, the company was the victim of a major cyber attack in 2023. This led to operational disruptions and cost the company over $350 million. While cyber attacks are rare for major public corporations, they can happen.

Largely the biggest risk for Clorox is declining volume growth with the persistent threat of private label. As private label products are often substantially cheaper than branded versions for very similar levels of quality, the company's volume gains will need to primarily come through product innovation and trustworthiness from the consumer.

That brings about a third risk in that consumer spending has to be viable enough to drive spending on branded goods. Low consumer confidence or spending ability will disable volume growth for Clorox. The company itself earlier this year even noted that 88% of consumers are exhibiting value-seeking behaviors.

Conclusion

Clorox, at face value, doesn't have a lot going for it in 2026. It is the normalization process of an outsized 2025 as the company recovered from a major cyberattack. With all of the movements and adjustments about to be behind them, the company is entering the back half of the fiscal year with momentum. As we look out to fiscal 2027, the profile of mid-single-digit top-line growth and double-digit earnings growth at an attractive valuation with a 5% dividend yield is very enticing and worth a look at from investors today.

data.title: Mastercard: The Market Is Pricing In Fear, But My Bull Case Points To $590
data.description:
Introduction

So far, this year has not been favorable to Financials, which is something that I find quite amusing since I view this as one of the most clearly identifiable beneficiaries of AI. As a result, guess what I have been buying of late, apart from Amazon (AMZN)? Stocks such as Mastercard (MA) and S&P Global (SPGI) are positions that I have been actively expanding in my portfolios. But I also think their sparring partners Visa (V) and Moody's (MCO) are great picks, as I explained in these two head-to-head comparisons:

  1. Moody's Vs. S&P Global
  2. Visa Vs. Mastercard

In a few days, Mastercard will report its Q1 2026 earnings; shortly after, Visa will have reported. There are a few things I think need to be considered before the print, and that is why I am writing this article.

Mastercard At A Glance

I bet everyone who is reading this article knows what Mastercard is. Allow me to spend a few words, however, on how I view it. Some of my readers know I particularly like infrastructure plays. If they are similar to toll booths, that is even better. These kinds of companies usually need to deploy billions in CapEx. After all, infrastructure is expensive to maintain. But then, there are digital infrastructure companies that combine the unique architectural role of infrastructure with the leaner financial structure of digital businesses. Mastercard is exactly one of these companies, as it owns one of the most important rails through which digital money flows around the world. By nature, it is a volume-driven business. Since digital payments are still not the number one choice in most countries around the world, Mastercard has a long-lasting opportunity to grow volumes. In addition to charging fees on gross dollar volume, switched transactions, and cross-border volume, it also charges a fee on currency conversion. However, the more it has developed its payment business, the more Mastercard has gathered data and developed a deep know-how on fraud detection, cybersecurity, tokenization, business insights, and similar. This has led to the creation of the value-added services and solutions business (VAS) that distinguishes Mastercard from its larger competitor, Visa. In case you don't know, roughly 60% of Mastercard's value-added services are network-linked, and this carries a big meaning with it because it shows the percentage of VAS revenues that grow organically with transaction volume growth. The advantage is clear: to sell its VAS, Mastercard doesn't truly need a separate sales cycle.

Mastercard's Key Economic Drivers

I wrote the paragraph above at the cost of sounding repetitive to many. But this is the direct ferry that carries us to the other side of the river, where we can deal with the main economic drivers that impact Mastercard. Consumer spending is the first, and in this situation, inflation is not a headwind as long as it doesn't truly affect consumption rates. This was recently discussed at the Wolfe Research FinTech forum, where, once again, we heard confirmation about healthy consumer spending. Secondly, cross-border travel is extremely important, as well as e-commerce volume. The digitalization of payments represents a tailwind that can act as a factor that partially offsets the cyclical downturns in spending, thanks to the cash-to-card conversion trend. Finally, FX also plays a role.

One of the reasons why payment rail giants such as Visa and Mastercard are underperforming this year is linked to stablecoins. I already explained how I view stablecoins as a tailwind, especially in Mastercard's case, which was further supported by Mastercard's Chief Commercial Payments Officer Raj Seshadri at the Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026, when she explained Mastercard's strategy in becoming the interoperability layer.

The recently announced BVNK acquisition, a stablecoin payments orchestrator, shows Mastercard's commitment to becoming a key player in this space. The acquisition makes sense because building from scratch a similar orchestrator internally would have taken years. Since stablecoins are expected to become real payment rails, even though they are not replacing cards, Mastercard wants to be present as the interoperability layer that sits between fiat money and tokenized money. It is basically what MA already does in the cards business: it acts as the bridge that connects different ecosystems by creating a trustworthy infrastructure with set rules, compliance standards, and a global reach. MA can monetize its stablecoin business in four ways: spread on send/convert/receive flows, FX, value-added services (MA's true cash cow), and wallet-as-a-service.

This acquisition is important because, traditionally, stablecoins are useful in high-friction flows, where MA was weak and, thus, undermonetized the opportunity. In addition, those who fear that MA will suffer from disintermediation can now see that MA is strengthening itself by integrating with those payment rails that should have been a threat. We have often heard MA say that it is rail-agnostic. Here we have further evidence of this.

Mastercard's Q1 2026 Earnings Preview

Last quarter, Mastercard guided for Q1 2026 net revenue growth of around 10%, which means that the implied revenue could be $8.3B. OpEx should be up 9% YoY, thus helping Mastercard's margin expansion trajectory. However, we also know that Q1 2026 will have a $200M one-time restructuring charge, which will have an impact on the company's EPS. Moreover, during the Q4 call, we were informed that January was a strong month with switched volume up 9% and cross-border up 13% YoY. However, things changed in March, and this leaves the biggest question mark over Mastercard's earnings. With the Middle East basically closed, I expect to see lower cross-border volume than previously anticipated. Therefore, I think that the role of VAS will be even more important. It is true that 60% is linked to volume growth, but if we see it consistently grow at a pace between 15% and 18%, then we would see the real structural strength of this new segment. If, on the other hand, the print reports VAS' growth below 15%, it will be a problem

What will matter even more will be the FY26 guidance. Currently, it targets a revenue growth in the high single digits. With Q2 on its way and Q3 coming, the two strongest cross-border quarters are going to be extremely important, and we will have to listen to what the management has to say in this regard. Interestingly, analysts are bullish, and we see that in the last three months, we have 16 EPS upward revisions versus only two downward revisions. However, 12 out of 17 analysts moderated their revenue revisions.

In a scenario where VAS reports revenue growth of 19% and cross-border volume grows at 12% (my base-case scenario), we could be expecting Q1 adj. EPS of $4.45 (the consensus is $4.41), which could lead to FY26 EPS of $20.00. In this case, applying a fwd earnings multiple of 25 gives us a price target of $500, which is, more or less, the price Mastercard trades at today. This would mean that the stock is more or less fairly priced. I want to be honest, though. When I say that I am buying Mastercard, it is because I am buying it for the bull case, where the main assumptions are cross-border volume growth of 14% and VAS growth of 23%. Even though Q1 and Q2 might see slower cross-border volume, I think we could be in accumulation territory because, once the Iran war ends, we could see a traffic rebound that will create a favorable setup for Mastercard. Here, I am using a 28x multiple, which, multiplied by the FY26 EPS of $21.00, gives me a target price close to $590, which is my current target.

As a result, I think Mastercard remains a very interesting buy ahead of earnings, with the addition that, in case of a dip after the report is out, it would become even more compelling.

data.title: Lowe's: Approaching Fair Value Amid Housing Weakness (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Lowe's Companies, Inc. (LOW) have been just a moderate performer over the past year, gaining about 9%. While the company has managed tariff headwinds fairly well, sales have been limited by a muted construction spending environment, both in terms of new construction as well as renovation and repair activity. At the same time, we have seen LOW be increasingly aggressive on the M&A front to catch up to Home Depot (HD) among “pro” customers. I last covered Lowe’s in January, when I reiterated the stock a “Sell,” and that call has played out with shares down 13% since then. With updated financials and a revised macro outlook, now is a good time to revisit Lowe’s.

Starting with the macro backdrop, while there were hopes of a recovery in the housing market in 2026, this looks increasingly unlikely to occur. After briefly dipping below 6%, mortgage rates have moved back up, with the upside risk to inflation likely postponing any Fed rate cuts. We will likely need to see 10-year yields sustainably below 4% to get mortgage rates below 6%, and that appears unlikely. Sure enough, existing home sales fell to a nine-month low in March. The level of housing transactions is deeply depressed at about 4 million units.

Many existing homeowners with low-rate mortgages are hesitant to move and take on a new mortgage while challenged affordability limits the pool of potential buyers. Plus, many post-2022 buyers have very limited home equity, making moving more difficult. Transaction levels are important for Lowe’s business as homeowners often do some work repairing homes to get them ready for sale while buyers may also take on some projects (from repainting a room to remodeling a bathroom) after buying the house. Fewer transactions limit this amount of activity. I believe existing home sales are basically “as bad as it gets,” but a recovery is unlikely until 2027.

With this channel closed, Lowe’s is dependent on existing homeowners undertaking projects on their own homes. Typically, these projects are more likely to occur during a period of home price appreciation, which is not the case in much of the nation, or low rates. Plus, while there are inevitable repair jobs that arise, many of these projects are discretionary and can be postponed when consumers feel less confident about the future. That appears to be happening.

Indeed, Lowe’s has seen ongoing weakness in do-it-yourself (“DIY”) activity, which is the company’s primary focus even as M&A has pushed it further into the Pro segment. Many renovation projects are discretionary. With interest rates elevated and affordability challenges front and center, many consumers are simply delaying big-ticket purchases and projects. With the affordability backdrop only having worsened over the past three months given the move higher in oil prices, I struggle to see this momentum shifting in 2026, especially with the spring/early summer so critical for construction-related activity.

Plus, its acquisitions of FBM and ADG have increased Lowe’s exposure to housing construction. As you can see below, the number of homes currently under construction has fallen for nearly four straight years, a prolonged recession in housing activity. Still, actual construction levels are still above pre-COVID norms. This speaks to the magnitude of the boom post-COVID when rates plunged and prices surged. Because of the level of new construction, many Sun Belt markets are still struggling with excess supply, weighing on prices. I expect Sun Belt pricing to remain negative this year, though we are seeing signs of some stabilization, and construction levels are unlikely to bottom until late 2026 in my view, remaining an ongoing drag on Lowe’s results.

The company last reported earnings on February 25, when Lowe’s reported a Q4 profit of $1.98, which beat estimates by $0.04. Same-store sales were up 1.3% as its aggressive investments in the Pro channel are helping to offset some of the pressures we have seen. Q4 was also when we saw rates decline and was before the start of the Iran War; as such, I am cautious that trends have worsened a bit since then. Gross margins declined 40 bps to 32.3%, and while tariff pressures should fade, I expect margins to remain muted in 2026 given lackluster consumer demand.

Additionally, Lowe’s ended the year with 3.3x debt/EBITDA. This was down 0.1x from Q3, and leverage should steadily improve as it uses free cash flow to pay down debt. LOW has a 2.75x leverage target, and I expect this to be achieved by Q3 2027, barring incremental M&A. With debt so far above target, I expect buybacks to be on hold for all of this year. Now, shares offer a 2% dividend yield. I view this payout as highly secure given Lowe’s cash flow.

Alongside results, the company offered a fairly downbeat outlook for the year. It expects sales to be up 7%-9%, aided by M&A, but EPS will be just $12.25-$12.75 as operating margins are expected to be just about 11.7%. Back in January, I was targeting $12.75-$12.90 in 2026 EPS, which was below consensus at the time. Lowe’s is offering an outlook even below that.

Even after its recent underperformance, Lowe’s shares continue to trade at a premium valuation; Seeking Alpha’s valuation screener rates shares a “D-.” As you can see below, the stock trades at a premium to the sector as well as to its own 5-year history, though not as pronounced of one as a few months ago. The market is still pricing in a fairly optimistic increase in housing-related spending this year, which I am cautious about. I also struggle with Lowe’s trading at a premium to itself given its over-leveraged balance sheet and paused share buybacks. While valuation alone may not be sufficient to drive a “Sell” rating, it precludes rushing to buy shares in my view.

Now, I do not see Lowe’s results getting worse in 2026 relative to 2025. Indeed, even if we saw a more meaningful decline in economic activity, I believe the downside in LOW’s earnings is fairly modest. We have basically endured a four-year housing recession, and in many areas, the level of activity is approaching levels that are as low as they can get. Metrics like existing home sales may not recover, but I struggle to see them falling much further.

Essentially, I see Lowe’s benefiting from a very tepid improvement in results, in part due to the integration of M&A and scaling up of its Pro offerings. I am looking for 2026 earnings of $12.40-$12.85, reflecting some deferral of DIY projects given inflation pressures and new construction levels that stabilize by late this year. That leaves shares just under 19x. Given the cyclicality of its business and the weakness of its balance sheet, I have felt 18x is a fair multiple, or $225-$230. This is about 5% below current levels, or ~3% including its dividend.

Frankly, I do not view a 3% downside as sufficient to merit an ongoing “Sell,” recognizing that no forecast is perfect. Shares are now close enough to fair value after their recent decline to be a “Hold” as the market has sufficiently deflated expectations around a housing recovery. That said, I would definitely not rush to buy LOW and would look elsewhere in the market for better opportunities. I would want to see shares pull back below $210 before there is enough upside to buy, given ongoing weakness in housing fundamentals, but after this drop, there is no pressing need to sell.

data.title: Kohl's: The Expected Tariff Refund Should Substantially Strengthen Its Financial Position
data.description:
Kohl's Corporation (KSS) Q4 2025 sales results were a bit weaker than I previously expected, but that was largely due to the temporary closure of many stores during the late January winter storms. Kohl's did well in terms of controlling its costs and still managed to report earnings above expectations despite that hit to sales.

Kohl's provided guidance for nearly flat net sales (and comparable store sales) during 2026, with guidance at -2% to 0% for both. Kohl's overall guidance was fairly close to my earlier expectations, but I believe that it will end up around the low end of its guidance due to the impact of the Middle East conflict.

Despite that, Kohl's is still expected to generate around $400 million in 2026 free cash flow after dividends (helped by a modest capex budget), while tariff refunds could add over $400 million more to its cash position.

I have increased my estimate of Kohl's value from $21 to $22 per share due to the expected tariff refund strengthening its financial position and giving it more room to weather the potential impact of the Middle East conflict on product costs and consumer spending.

Q4 2025 Results

Kohl's reported -2.8% comparable store sales in Q4 2025, which was a minor sequential decline from the -1.7% comparable store sales that it reported in Q3 2025. This resulted in Kohl's reporting -3.1% full-year comparable store sales.

This sales performance in Q4 was slightly worse than Kohl's expected, given that it updated (in Q3 2025) its full-year guidance to a range of -2.5% to -3.0%. This implies a roughly 1% miss in Q4 2025 compared to the guidance midpoint. Much of this appears to be attributable to severe winter weather in late January 2026, which resulted in the temporary closure of around half of Kohl's stores and a 70 basis point hit to its Q4 2025 comps.

Despite that slight sales weakness, Kohl's managed to deliver operating margins above expectations due to strong cost controls. Kohl's reported 3.3% adjusted operating margins for the full year, versus its updated expectations for 3.1% to 3.2% adjusted operating margins. Kohl's gross margins ended up 34 basis points compared to 2024, and its SG&A dollars were down -4.1% compared to 2024 (although as a percentage of revenues, it was very close to 2024 levels).

This helped Kohl's to report adjusted diluted EPS of $1.62 for the full year, above its guidance of $1.25 to $1.45.

Tariff Refunds

A positive item is that Kohl's appears to be on track to receive a significant amount of tariff refunds (estimated at $550 million). This may result in a roughly $430 million post-tax boost to Kohl's cash position.

This would give Kohl's some room to allow for slightly lower margins and increased promotional activity to appeal to customers seeking value in the current environment.

2026 Outlook (Excluding Tariff Refunds)

Kohl's provided guidance for -2% to flat net sales and comparable store sales growth in 2026, along with adjusted diluted EPS in the $1.00 to $1.60 range. My prior expectation for Kohl's was that it could do around flat to +1% comparable store sales in 2026 along with approximately $1.45 per share in adjusted diluted EPS. Thus Kohl's 2026 guidance was pretty close to my expectations, although modestly weaker (by around 1.5% in terms of sales growth and $0.15 in terms of adjusted diluted EPS) at guidance midpoint.

Kohl's has done relatively well recently in terms of managing its costs and reducing its comparable store sales decline to minimal levels. However, I believe that the Middle East conflict will put some pressure on Kohl's business.

This pressure would come both from increased product costs and some caution from consumers around discretionary spending with higher gasoline costs. Kohl's recognizes the need to provide value to its consumers, so I am assuming that it might sacrifice a bit of its margins to keep the price of affected products attractive to its customers.

I am now modeling Kohl's 2026 results with -2% net sales and gross margins of 37.2% (compared to 37.5% in 2026). This reflects the impact of the Middle East conflict on Kohl's results.

Kohl's projected adjusted operating margins would be 2.8%, around the low end of guidance, while adjusted diluted EPS would be around $0.80 to $0.85 in this scenario.

$ Million

Net Sales

$14,480

Other Revenue

$715

Cost of Merchandise Sold

$9,090

SG&A

$5,000

Depreciation and Amortization

$700

Interest Expense

$285

Income Tax

$25

Net Income

$95

Free Cash Flow

Kohl's mentioned that it expects 2026 capex to be around $350 million to $400 million, while inventory is expected to be down low-to-mid single digits.

Thus, Kohl's should be able to generate approximately $400 million in free cash flow after dividends in 2026 even if its results end up around the low end of guidance.

This doesn't include the tariff refunds. Kohl's net cash position could end up going up by over $800 million in 2026, including the refunds.

Estimated Valuation

Kohl's 2026 guidance was close to my expectations, and management has executed well recently. However, as noted above, I am wary about external factors contributing to Kohl's results ending up towards the lower end of its guidance. As well, there is the potential for the prolonged Middle East conflict to affect Kohl's 2027 results as well.

On the other hand, the projected tariff refund may add over $3.50 per share to Kohl's cash position.

Thus I am increasing my estimate of Kohl's value from $21 to $22 per share, assuming that tax refund comes in as projected.

Conclusion

Kohl's has managed to largely stabilize its sales, with guidance for -2% to flat sales in 2026. I am modeling Kohl's to end up around the low end of guidance due to the impact of the Middle East conflict. However, at the low end of guidance, Kohl's can still generate around $400 million in free cash flow after dividends.

Kohl's may also receive over $400 million (after-tax) from tariff refunds, helping to significantly strengthen its financial position.

I have slightly increased my estimate of Kohl's value from $21 to $22 per share to reflect its improved financial position, despite my belief that its 2026 results will be a bit weaker than it expects (at guidance midpoint).

data.title: Iran: The Economic Damage Is Cumulative
data.description:
Today marks the two-month anniversary of the conflict in Iran. A ceasefire continues to hold with some minor transgressions. However, peace talks have yielded little progress to this point, and the critical global chokepoint of the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to most traffic.

This week will be dominated by first quarter results flowing rapidly across the wires. Over 40% of S&P 500 companies are scheduled to post Q1 numbers and guidance during the week. However, over the next month, nothing will be nearly as important as getting the Strait of Hormuz reopened as far as the markets and the global economy go.

The shutdown of this critical global chokepoint has resulted in a huge surge in prices in many key energy and commodity products. Average gasoline prices have moved to over $4 a gallon for the first time since 2022. Diesel prices have shot up by some 50%. And nearly every locomotive and trailer truck runs on diesel. This boosts the costs of moving any goods to the market.

U.S. consumers were already in a foul mood before the latest war in the Middle East. This latest headwind to consumer health is already showing up in polling. Americans who rated the economy poor jumped in April compared to March.

And there is U.S. consumer sentiment, which just hit its lowest point in April since this metric started to be recorded in 1978. Lower than the trough during the Great Financial Crisis. Yet, the market is at all-time highs.

And there is no easy path to a resolution to this conflict in the weeks ahead. Regime change in Iran simply isn't going to happen via airpower alone, as it never has in history. That leaves "boots on the ground," which is a non-starter politically in the United States. A full-scale invasion is impossible without reinstituting a draft (another political non-starter), given Iran has some 95 million citizens. That is more than 50 million people than Iraq in comparison.

The current Iranian leadership has stated it will not negotiate peace until the current U.S. blockade of its ports is lifted. Another potential political non-starter. So, what we have here is the classic Mexican standoff. Each week the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, the more inflation pressures build throughout the world and the probability of global recession increases. There have been some workarounds, such as the east/west pipeline in Saudi Arabia that has mitigated the over 20 million barrels a day of crude that usually flows through the Strait of Hormuz. That said, Goldman Sachs has estimated that Gulf crude production is now down ~14.5 million barrels per day, or -57% versus pre-war levels.

On the flip side, oil storage capacity in Iran is rapidly running out. This means production will need to start to be shut down in the country. JPMorgan recently stated this will happen over the next two to three weeks. This will cause these fields to be permanently impaired to some extent. So, who blinks first in this conflict? Currently, Polymarket is assigning just a 43% probability of a permanent peace deal being completed by the end of June.

And here is the point most investors seem to be missing right now. And that is even if the Strait of Hormuz miraculously reopened next week; the economic damage of this war has been cumulative and will continue to negatively impact both the global economy and inflation levels throughout the rest of 2026.

And think of all the tasks that follow a peace agreement that need to happen for things to "normalize." Mines will have to be cleared from the Strait of Hormuz. This will take several weeks or months. Strategic Petroleum Reserves in Europe, the United States, and China will need to be replenished. This will push up oil demand for several quarters. And if Iran has to shut down production in the coming weeks, some global supply will be knocked offline permanently or will take significant time to bring back to previous production levels.

Dozens of refineries, chemical plants, and other key energy facilities in the Gulf regions have been significantly damaged or destroyed. This will take tens of billions of dollars to fully repair, and this work will take months or years in some cases. Billions of dollars of damage to U.S. military bases in the region has also been done. The U.S. military has requested $200 billion of supplemental funds to cover this war. The administration also plans on the biggest annual percentage increase to defense spending in FY2027 since WWII. This is hardly what the federal budget needed, as fiscal deficits were already close to running at $2 trillion annually and overall federal debt is closing in on $40 trillion.

The blockage to the Strait of Hormuz has spiked fertilizer prices globally. This will cause food insecurity in much of the third world, and that situation will worsen each week these supplies are shut in. Here in the U.S., these headwinds are hitting right during spring planting season. Parts of the Midwest, the breadbasket of the United States, also face significant drought conditions; it should also be noted. It is difficult to see how food inflation does not increase significantly in the months ahead given these factors.

Goldman Sachs just raised its baseline projection, calling for an average of $90 Brent oil in the fourth quarter, as it factors in how long it will take to normalize global oil supply/demand dynamics from this conflict. So, where does this leave us? The longer the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, the higher the probability that parts of Europe and Asia go into recession.

Given the U.S. is much less dependent on the Middle East and is largely energy independent, the country should be able to avoid a recession as long as spending for AI infrastructure remains so robust. That said, these factors should slow economic growth while at the same time boosting inflation levels, a recipe for stagflation. The CEO of Ducat Maritime stated recently that "the ongoing disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz are creating inflationary pressures equivalent to roughly half of what was experienced during the COVID-19 pandemic."

The $64,000 question for investors is, are these potential scenarios priced into the markets at current valuation levels? My own opinion is they are not, and this is why I remain conservatively positioned (30% short-term treasuries & 70% covered call holdings) within my own portfolio.

data.title: FINX: AI Is A Boon, X Money's 6% APY Is A Threat
data.description:
Thesis

Artificial Intelligence should lead to many improvements across the finance industry, impacting both traditional banks and newer fintech companies. I believe the possibilities for concrete, substantial positive disruptions are especially high in fintech and the larger finance industry. The potential for AI combined with a well-diversified portfolio sporting solid fundamentals earns the Global X FinTech ETF (FINX) a buy rating from me, but I would keep stakes moderate. I also provides exposure to some companies like PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) and Block, Inc. (XYZ), which have been struggling, and that could weigh this portfolio down.

Speaking of disruptions, a new entrant may rattle some nerves and force the competition to step up. Elon Musk has been testing X Money, a payment app built on the X platform. During testing, 6% APY savings accounts were offered to users. If X Money offers those rates to the wider population, it could draw in quite a few customers and force competitors to raise interest rates.

FinTech Could Be One Of The Biggest Beneficiaries Of AI

One of the most important considerations for investors these days is when, how, and where AI will prove disruptive. Even if you think that AI as a whole is a bubble, I still believe this consideration is important. AI is often compared to the dot-com bubble. Yet while the dot-com bubble did wipe out a lot of companies, the Internet grew, and many web-centric organizations, like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), ultimately thrived. Likewise, an AI bubble popping might wipe out a lot of companies, but AI as a technology could still prove disruptive in the long run.

Some key ways AI could benefit fintech include:

  • Perhaps no industry is as heavily impacted by fraud as the financial industry. Targeting and utilizing modern fintech apps is quite common these days. AI could actually be a threat on this front, empowering criminals. In the long run, however, I believe financial companies can out-resource those who would cause harm. AI can be used to find bugs, detect unusual activity, and for various other things.

  • Making common business-oriented language (COBOL), the programming language that much of the world’s financial infrastructure relies on, more affordable and accessible is important. I believe modern tech apps, like Venmo, are not as reliant as COBOL, but the language is still a major part of the larger financial system.

  • While many fintech companies eschew brick-and-mortar locations, thus gaining an edge over traditional banks, other “old school” costs, like customer service, remain high. AI should be able to lower many of these costs, although I expect customer service to remain human-centric for quite some time.

  • Fintech companies can have especially high software development and maintenance costs. This is an area that AI is already impacting. AI chatbots might replace traditional search engines, and agentic AI may require fewer SaaS seats for various applications. However, I don’t see AI or agents outright replacing the traditional banking system.
  • Credit scoring is complex and can be inaccurate. AI should be able to conduct better analysis. It could be especially helpful with clients who have minimal history.

I don’t think the above list is exhaustive. Far from it. Quite likely, AI will impact the financial industry and human society as a whole in ways we can’t yet anticipate. If AI is as potent as many tech gurus believe, fintech and the larger finance industry could be transformed.

Near-Term Catalyst To Pay Especially Close Attention To

FinTech strikes me as a high-upside but low-floor endeavor. That’s part of what makes FINX attractive to me, as it casts a wider net. There are some holdings I would be wary of making substantial investments in individually, except perhaps as risk capital stakes. That said, in some cases, developments in AI could mitigate some of the more serious risks.

Buy Now, Pay Later lender Affirm Holdings, Inc.’s (AFRM) engagement with borrowers with thin or less desirable credit histories, for example, presents considerable risks. Affirm is already using AI for more nuanced real-time scoring for potential borrowers, and so far its reasonable charge-off rates lend hope that their approach is effective. I’m not quite ready to grade Affirm as a buy on its own, but I’m coming around enough that it’s fine in an ETF.

The health of consumers has been a pretty hot topic as of late. For fintech lenders, financially constrained consumers are a double-edged sword. Lending should continue to rise as people relieve financial strains, but it is important to be mindful of any breaking points. AI scoring should help, but that doesn't mean greater market risks are eliminated.

The number of low- and moderate-income investors has risen by 167%, which should benefit companies like Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD). I last rated Robinhood as a hold back in September, worrying primarily over valuation concerns, and the stock has slid more than 30%. I’d have to do another full analysis to offer a grade for HOOD specifically, but I’d be surprised if I didn’t come away with at least a confident risk capital bet and wouldn’t be surprised with a full-fledged buy rating. Revenue growth remains strong, and HOOD strikes me as firmly established with its target audiences.

In general, I believe the fintech industry as a whole can encourage access and utilization among communities that traditional banking may have overlooked in the past. Robinhood is a perfect example of that. Fintech firms are also showing high adoption with small businesses. Toast has been recording blistering growth. With many small restaurants and other establishments facing cost pressure, services that can deliver savings and efficiency should enjoy solid tailwinds.

The above points aren't exhaustive. Fintech is quite complex and nuanced, and there is a lot to consider. This is especially true with emerging developments, like AI implementation. This is part of why an ETF looks especially attractive.

X Money: The Disruptive Potential Is Hard To Ignore

Money makes the world go round. As such, it’s not much of a surprise that Elon Musk is looking to disrupt the finance sector. While I am skeptical of the valuations Musk’s companies carry, I do respect his disruptive potential. Musk has been angling to turn X (formerly known as Twitter) into a so-called “everything” app, and it looks like banking will be one of his opening moves.

I was quite skeptical when I first heard about X Money. Actually, I’m still rather skeptical now, but there is one thing that caught my eye: a 6% APY. At least in testing, X Money offered users a savings account with a 6% yield. If this is offered nationwide to users, it could disrupt the fintech space and also potentially spark an interest rate war as competing banks and payment apps are spurred to increase rates.

In regard to Musk’s ambitions, a simple payment app is no longer enough. There are already a plethora of said apps available. In the investing space, old-school brokerages are well entrenched, and more disruptive companies like Robinhood will be difficult to overcome.

A 6% yield, however, could draw in users. How will X Money be able to pay that? That’s the question that needs answering. As a digital-only banking service, X Money should have substantially lower overhead than traditional banks. The advantage over other fintech apps, however, might be minimal. If the company can build enough profit engines by becoming an everything banking app, a 6% rate should be doable and profitable.

I’ll remain in wait-and-see mode, but if Musk and company deliver and X Money looks primed for success, I would have to closely consider my buy rating for FINX. X Money could launch a user acquisition war, luring away customers, which in turn could force other fintechs and banking services to up interest rates.

FINX Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

Surprise, surprise, FINX’s portfolio is largely made up of tech and finance companies. Its market-cap methodology also includes a maximum 6% weight for holdings and a 40% cap on the aggregate weight of securities with over 5% allocation. There are 75 equity holdings in the portfolio, according to Morningstar. One aspect I like about this ETF is that none of the holdings top 7% (before rebalancing). The biggest single holding is PayPal at 6.32%. I’m rather leery of PayPal, but not enough that a 6% holding would scare me off.

Interestingly, the portfolio P/E ratio of 14.50 is way below what's becoming common in the tech industry. That said, the financial/banking industry often does have low P/E ratios. Handling cash for end users can result in liabilities on the books, and financial companies are often highly leveraged. This, in turn, increases risks, which are then priced into the stock. Fintech firms tend to operate differently from traditional banks but, in some cases, may have to deal with similar burdens, such as liabilities created by holding a user's cash.

The 1.88 Price/Sales ratio (Morningstar data) is also quite attractive and very low compared to non-financial tech companies, where ratios can easily top 8. Unfortunately, sales growth for the portfolio comes in at just under 10%. With non-finance tech companies, sales growth in excess of 20% is pretty common. The TTM dividend yield is just .66%, so if you’re looking for an income ETF, look elsewhere.

I am not a fan of the .68% adjusted expense ratio and would have liked to see it closer to .5%. Still, fintech is rather complicated, and building a portfolio with +70 equity holdings is going to take a lot of work. Tracking errors are hovering around 5%, which is maybe a touch high but nothing I would lose sleep over.

Why I Could Be Wrong

First, AI could fall short of the promise. Plenty of people believe just that. Personally, I think AI will go down as one of the most important technologies in history, but I could be wrong. Also, with finance specifically, the gains offered by AI, like the ability to detect fraud, may be offset by criminals, and especially, AI-enabled criminals.

I’m mildly skeptical of X Money. It’s a solid idea, but the market is already crowded. That said, Elon Musk has proven doubters wrong before. While I am skeptical, I wouldn’t be shocked if X Money succeeds. If Musk can disrupt fintech, some current companies will likely lose out, and even the most competitive rivals would have to up their game.

Key Takeaway: The AI Disruption Potential Is Enticing

So far, the real-world economic impact of AI seems to be quite muted outside of software development, defense, and a few other areas. I don't think this is due to limitations in the technology but instead because these industries were the highest priorities. AI in software development will impact software development across every industry and may supercharge AI development itself. Defense is always a priority. And I suspect that fintech (and traditional banking, for that matter) will be high on the list of industries to focus on going forward. The AI disruption potential is high enough to earn a soft buy rating from me.

There are some risky holdings in this portfolio, but with risk comes upside, and spreading around investments with a well-diversified portfolio should mitigate the risks to some extent. I am bullish on fintech, especially given AI, but there is one important caveat: my outlook remains positive so long as consumers continue to show resilience. I have concerns about the health of consumers, but so far, they have shown resilience in the face of considerable headwinds. If consumer health starts to deteriorate, I will probably be among the quickest to turn bearish regarding the whole market, and fintech would probably be one of the areas I'd consider cutting quickly. That said, the fintech industry would also probably be one of the sectors I'd examine especially closely if I feel markets are bottoming out. I would expect the drop with fintech to be steep, but the rebound could turn out to be very high in this scenario.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The New Economic Law: Lower Interest Rates When Inflation Rises
data.description:
One of the first concepts you learn when you study economics is the central bank’s behavior when the inflation rate varies greatly from the 2% target. When it gets higher, central banks are supposed to increase interest rates to preserve the value of the currency; when it goes down, they are supposed to decrease interest rates to stimulate the economy and avoid a recession. These are the underlying mechanisms followed by major central banks over the last few decades, but what if this basic relationship no longer holds up?

Central Banks Have No Room to Maneuver

According to the IEA Chief, the world is facing the biggest energy security threat in history. The Strait of Hormuz has been closed for about 2 months in a row, and that’s a disaster for global trade. It is not just a matter of paying more to fill up your car, but a global problem in terms of future food supply (fertilizer prices are skyrocketing) and critical elements shortage like helium, a crucial component to manufacture semiconductors.

The prolonged closure of this chokepoint is having significant consequences on global trade, and the worst part is that we don’t know yet when it will be reopened. Over the last weekend, Iranian officials, once again, didn’t want to negotiate in person with US officials, which is why the latter decided not to fly to Pakistan. President Trump doesn’t feel the pressure to reach an agreement anytime soon (“we have no pressure”), and the same goes for Iran, not willing to open the Strait until its demanding conditions are accepted by the US. Basically, the war is in a stalemate, but the Strait remains closed and threatens to disrupt even more the global economy.

Following the law of demand and supply, when inelastic goods like energy, food, and critical elements for semiconductors face a shortage, general prices must inevitably go up, driving the inflation rate way higher than the 2% target. Therefore, as long as this Strait remains closed, I believe it is reasonable to expect a fast rise of the inflation rate well above the 2% target. In fact, March data support my thesis.

US headline CPI rose 0.90% MoM (the biggest increase since July 2022), driven by significantly higher gasoline prices. Germany's CPI increased by 1.10% MoM (the biggest increase since October 2022); the UK recorded a CPI expansion of 0.70% MoM. These are just March data; in a couple of days we are going to have the preliminary data for April, the second month in a row in which the Strait remained closed. The upward inflationary pressure is already quite evident, and it will increase if the war doesn’t end anytime soon, something that I consider unlikely at this point.

Anyway, how problematic would a new inflationary wave be now? By itself, a high inflation rate is not an unbeatable enemy, and the 1970s/early 1980s prove it.

Back then, because of the oil embargo, the inflation rate passed twice the 10% threshold, and the whole decade suffered from high inflation. In the end, Paul Volcker (12th chairman of the Federal Reserve) solved the problem by bringing the prime rate close to 20%, ultimately causing a severe recession. It was a bitter medicine but needed; the S&P 500 Index experienced a lost decade.

Could the Fed today follow a similar approach in case of high inflation? In my opinion, absolutely not, for one simple reason: the current global economy is buried in debt.

Back in the early 1980s, the US Debt/GDP ratio was 30%; now it is four times as much. If you hike interest rates when the economy is highly leveraged, the snowball effect on the government interest payments is much more severe. To put things in perspective, on a $10 trillion debt (roughly the amount that needs to be refinanced in 2026), a 2% interest rate equals $200 billion in annual interest payments; at 5%, it means paying $500 billion, and at 8%, it means paying $800 billion, almost close to the total military spending in 2025 ($954 billion). In addition, $10 trillion represents just a fourth of the total US debt; therefore, if prolonged, the magnitude of this problem could be even greater.

Compared to 1976, mandatory and net interest spending is taking up most of the US GDP, which means less funds for education, transportation, defense, and research.

Overall, assuming the current energy crisis will lead to a much higher inflation rate than the 2% target, central banks won’t have the same room to maneuver they had in the 1970s/1980s. There is too much debt, and it is not possible to repeat Volcker’s approach in the early 1980s even if needed. Times have changed compared to fifty years ago.

At this point, someone might think that we have already recovered from a relatively recent energy crisis (2022), and central banks could apply the same approach to fight the current one. Well, I believe there are important differences that won’t make it happen.

The first one is that the current energy crisis is not even comparable to what we experienced in 2022: we are talking about a daily oil deficit of 13 million barrels.

If the Strait remains closed, in 30 days our lives might be affected in ways we couldn’t even imagine. Besides the pump prices, lots of flights scheduled for the summer session could be cancelled; the EU has already made several warnings about it. Businesses that can no longer sustain higher energy prices might be forced to shut down, and more countries might declare a state of national energy emergency.

Second, the labor market is still solid but weaker compared to 2022. If you just look at the unemployment rate, you won’t notice a big difference. After all, when the Fed started to raise interest rates in March 2022, the unemployment rate was 3.70%; now it is 4.30%. However, to really understand the current state of the labor market, you need to take into account the composition of it, not just a single number.

The chart above shows you full-time workers (blue line) and part-time workers (red line) as a percentage of the total employment level. As you can see, since 2022, full-time workers started to decline while part-time workers experienced the opposite; this kind of trend is typical during recessions. Furthermore, the job openings declined by a wide margin since 2022.

March 2022 was the peak moment if you wanted to find a new job; now it is quite challenging; AI adoption might have played a major role.

Overall, compared to 2022, full-time jobs have been replaced by part-time jobs, and looking for a new job is getting harder. In this context, rising interest rates could be the final blow for the economy. As we all know, the Fed has a dual mandate (maximum employment and stable prices), but I believe the top priority is now employment. In this regard, Kevin Warsh's nomination as Federal Reserve chairman seems to be heading in that direction.

The Energy Crisis Does Not Seem to Worry Warsh

President Trump has never hidden his preference for a lower interest rate environment; therefore, it makes a lot of sense to think that he had all the interest in appointing a new Fed Chair in line with his view. There is nothing strange about it, and based on Kevin Warsh's recent statements, I believe he is definitely in line with his view. In particular, two of them deserve a deep dive.

When asked by Senator Kim regarding the ongoing energy crisis and the potential consequences on the economy and general prices, this was Warsh’s answer:

Senator, if my reform agenda, if confirmed stands for anything, it's for the central bank, especially the Fed chairman to stay in its lane, so I wouldn't want to wander outside of it. The Fed should have an open mind towards all sorts of data, but the things that central banks can affect are things that are not one off in nature, but are persistent and find their way into the generalized price level, and that would be my focus.

Based on my interpretation, it seems to me that Warsh doesn’t consider the current conflict in the Middle East as a Fed priority. The reason is pretty simple: this conflict is supposed to deliver “one-off” effects on prices, and the Fed should focus on things that are persistent in nature. Once the war is over, prices are going to stabilize. In other words, if the inflation rate gets way higher than 2% because of rising energy prices, the Fed shouldn’t raise interest rates, or at least this is my interpretation of his speech.

According to his view, the Fed should focus on aspects that affect the long-term trend of general prices, AI implementation above all. In fact, since AI is a long-term deflationary force, he believes that it could even lay the groundwork for a lower rate environment. So, what does that mean for us investors?

It means that even though we are facing the worst energy crisis ever and the inflation rate might soon reach 4-5%, the next Fed Chair will still be willing to cut interest rates (therefore lowering real interest rates). Of course, monetary policy is not just up to him; the whole FOMC decides on it, but the Chair always has a major influence.

At this point a question arises: won't lowering interest rates during an energy crisis accelerate the inflationary spiral even more? In my opinion it will, but I have some doubts that all this will be reflected in the inflation rate. One of the most discussed topics regarding Warsh is his willingness to change the inflation rate calculation.

What I’m most interested in is: What’s the underlying inflation rate? Not: What’s the one-time change in prices because of a change in geopolitics or change in beef?

The measures I prefer are looking at things that are called trimmed averages. We take out all of the tail-risks, all of the one-off items, and we ask ourselves whether the generalized change in prices is having second-order effects on the economy.

Basically, Warsh wants an inflation rate that excludes outlier data, such as a massive increase in energy prices because of the war in the Middle East. In his opinion, leaving out the tail risk better reflects the current situation, but I couldn’t disagree more.

If beef prices significantly increase because of the war in the Middle East, it definitely matters, as consumers are going to notice it; just because the increase is not included in a spreadsheet, it doesn’t mean that it doesn’t exist. Of course, the same goes for everything else. Households' spending (~ 68% of GDP) doesn’t care if the inflation rate is 2% on paper if everything is much more expensive than before. Eventually, the economy will suffer a major setback due to the lower consumer spending. By the way, once prices go up, they don’t come back to the previous level; at best, they increase at a slower rate than before, so “one-off events” do have a permanent effect on the economy.

Conclusion

Overall, Warsh’s appointment seems in line with a high inflationary environment, regardless of how the inflation rate will be calculated in the future.

If my assumption is correct, for us investors this means favoring short-term bonds over long-term bonds and relying on safe-haven assets like gold in the medium-long term; the latter is one of the best-performing assets during periods of negative real rates, and this is where I believe we are heading. However, since oil prices are on the rise, gold might keep suffering in the short term.

As we have seen before, stocks tend to struggle when the inflation rate is high, and that’s a current major risk since we are close to all-time highs. The market is priced for perfection, as if the war in the Middle East already belongs to the past, but an agreement between the US and Iran is still missing. Meanwhile, the Strait of Hormuz enters its 60th day of closure, and oil prices just got back to triple digits.

data.title: Nike: A Buy Into The Window That Has Never Failed
data.description:
A few months ago, I published a data-driven analysis of the performance of Nike, Inc. (NKE) and Adidas (ADDYY) over varying timelines tied to the FIFA World Cup cycle.

For Nike, a combination of tariffs, a longer inventory-clearing cycle, and a China slump has meant that the historical precedent for buying one year and six months before the World Cup kick-off looks essentially broken for the 2026 cycle. So far it is down 31%-33% on those timelines (to date, returns on the stock price on Jun 11, 2025, and Dec 11, 2025). That makes the next window quite interesting, as “a month before kick-off” is also the most statistically significant entry point in the data. While this window has never failed, it requires a deeper look at its recovery bridge.

The $3 EPS Bridge Is the Whole Nike Thesis

Nike’s diluted EPS was consistently above $3.00 during the FY21 – FY24 years. Compare this to the FY26 and FY27 estimates of $1.49 and $1.87, and you can explain much of the drawdown in the stock. So, the key question is whether Nike can rebuild the bridge to its $3.00 EPS by FY28. If it can, a 20-22x multiple would support a price of $60-$66, a nearly 50% return.

You saw the early signs of those actions this quarter, and it is a critical step in our return to double-digit EBIT margins - Elliott Hill, CEO, President & Director of Nike at the Q3 earnings call.

We expect gross margin expansion to begin in the second quarter due to actions to mitigate tariffs and recovery of transitory impacts from Win Now – Matthew Friend, Nike CFO at the Q3 earnings call.

Nike’s EBIT margins are at 7.1% in the first 9 months of FY26 against its publicly stated goal of double-digit EBIT margins. That requires approximately 300 bps of margin expansion. A lot of the margin compression recently is attributable to tariffs in North America with a 650 bps hit in Q3. A 300-bps margin expansion would mean an additional $1.4 billion flowing through the bottom line. That nearly closes the gap to a $3.00 EPS. The remainder must come from a return to at least a mid-single-digit growth in revenue. Without it, even the brand moat wouldn’t help arrest a compression in its multiple.

Parallels to the Adidas Resurgence in 2014

In 2014, Adidas (ADDDF) was struggling in the US after being pushed to the No. 3 spot in the athletic apparel market. However, just two years later, it was growing faster than both Nike and Under Armour (UA). That success was built around its Boost technology and winning cultural needle-movers like Kanye West and Pharrell Williams. The creator economy today is far more fragmented compared to the Yeezy era. Therefore, it seems nearly impossible to find that part of the formula. The mechanical benefits from cushioning technology are also in the diminishing returns territory.

However, Nike’s recent Mind 001 and Mind 002 releases, which draw from neuroscience, indicate the contrary, that the company does not have an innovation fatigue problem. These recovery slides, as they are called, were sold out at launch and are trading at two to three times the price on reseller websites. The CEO in the Q3 earnings call indicated that there were 2 million customers who signed up for “notify me” on the website. While still early, this category-creating playbook does suggest that the innovation engine still has life, and it can pull consumers around a product without a celebrity-driven scarcity. More importantly, these slides are priced at a more reasonable $95 - $150 price range, which leaves room for broader adoption.

China is Priced, But Still Presents Risks

Management has already indicated that the China problem is structural, with guidance of a 20% decline in the fourth quarter. The damage from this regional weakness will be visible on the overall growth rate. The headlines indicate that to be a major reason for a dip in the stock.

However, running still grew in double digits, and it is the lifestyle segment that dragged. A reasonable conclusion here is that the market is maturing towards functional gear and shunning status products. This is a harder problem, and for the stock to work from its current levels, it does need to present evidence that the China problem will be contained beyond FY27. This is a fundamental risk to the stock, as further declines would mean that growth in revenue would be harder to achieve.

Clustered Insider Conviction

In the days following the Q3 print and Wall Street downgrades, four insiders bought the stock in the open market. The Form 4 filings show the transaction code P, or an open market purchase. There can be many reasons for why insiders sell their stock. But there’s only one reason to buy their stock, and that is to make money. Therefore, such purchases are often associated with abnormal returns.

Apart from the clustered timing of the buys, there is an additional reason why this could be an informationally dense signal. The percentage increase in their stakes ranges from 10% to 27%. So, these may not be token boardroom buys.

Conclusion

The most profitable entries can be found when sentiments are at their lowest and catalysts like the World Cup are at their peak. Despite that, a high-conviction Strong Buy is incompatible when you line it up against structural risks. Neither is a downgrade when you consider the recovery bridge to be reasonable. I am therefore maintaining a Buy rating on Nike.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XLB: Materials' Alpha Cools As The U.S.-Iran Conflict Presses On
data.description:
The resource trade was hot to begin 2026. Energy and Materials rose sharply, first on the back of gold and silver and then ahead of the conflict in Iran, as WTI and Brent crude oil climbed. But in the past two months, the State Street Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB) has struggled, both on an absolute and relative basis.

I had a hold rating on XLB back in November of 2025. Shares are up 20% since then, outperforming the S&P 500 by some 14%. Still, the valuation has risen, with most of the US market’s upward EPS revisions being seen in the Information Technology sector. With unconvincing technicals, I reiterate a hold rating. I’ll provide a fresh look at XLB’s fundamentals, valuation, and technicals.

XLB Now Lags SPY YoY

S&P 500 Sector Valuations

According to the issuer, XLB "seeks to provide precise exposure to companies in the chemical, construction material, containers and packaging, metals and mining, and paper and forest products industries." Tracking the S&P 500 Materials sector index, the fund allows investors to take strategic or tactical positions at a more targeted level than traditional style-based investing.

XLB is a large ETF with $7.5 billion in assets under management as of April 27, 2026. That’s up from $5.3 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Its annual expense ratio is low at just 8 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is about 0.4 percentage points above that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum is solid right now, earning XLB a B ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. Of course, Materials is among the riskiest and most cyclically sensitive corners of the stock market; hence, the ETF Risk Grade is a weak D+. Finally, on liquidity, the fund scores well, given an average daily volume of more than 16 million shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of just 2 basis points, per State Street Funds.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar plots across the top two rows of the style box. It’s virtually entirely a mid- and large-cap product, with an ever-so-modest bent to value over growth. While there is no international exposure (being a US sector ETF), Materials equities tend to benefit from a weaker US dollar (like ex-US stocks). As plotted in the chart above, the greenback has been remarkably quiet in the past 10 months or so.

On valuation, XLB now trades at a below-market 19.0x price-to-earnings ratio. The earnings multiple is up a couple of figures since my November 2025 assessment, while the long-term EPS growth rate has actually ticked up. The PEG ratio is virtually unchanged, so I’m sticking with a hold rating.

XLB: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, the 100% Materials sector product has high exposure to its top 10 positions. Linde plc (LIN) commands the highest weight, now close to 15%. Newmont Corporation (NEM) is a gold miner, and it has stumbled since notching highs earlier this year. Gold and silver have become highly tied to the S&P 500, so the diversification benefit from shares of companies in that industry is not all that solid right now.

Where XLB did help from a portfolio perspective was with its access to agricultural and fertilizer names during the Iran war outbreak. But even those stocks have cooled.

Holdings And Dividend Information

From a seasonal standpoint, XLB has tended to produce decent returns now through August. July has been particularly strong (second only to November in terms of average price gains over the past 10 years).

Bullish Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With a fair valuation and concentrated allocation, XLB’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that the ETF put in a record high back in the first quarter. A steep March drop reminded investors that risk can happen fast. Just a few weeks later, however, XLB successfully tested its long-term 200-day moving average, eventually returning to right near February’s range highs. The 200dma remains on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

But the ETF met selling pressure at the base of the previous range and now may be rolling over as the 50dma turns down. That’s not the ideal setup, despite a capitulation-like RSI figure at the March nadir. Long-term support is down just below $47, with resistance from $52 to $54. The volume profile interests me, since we have seen the number of shares traded fall as the consolidation has played out. That often means the previous trend (up) is still intact.

Fails At The February High, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on XLB. The Materials sector ETF is not all that compelling on valuation, and it has turned very sensitive to the conflict in Iran and domestic macroeconomic forecast changes. Its chart is also more of a show-me story than anything as we progress through the Q1 earnings season.

data.title: Nvidia: AI Transcends Semiconductors' Cyclical Nature
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) is up by 21% since my last analysis of the company in March, in which I issued a Buy rating. Some argue that Nvidia is near a cyclical peak, but I think they will be proven wrong. I heard Elon Musk say something interesting the other day, and I'm paraphrasing strongly, but the notion was essentially AI growing in multiple s-curves, which I believe should logically reflect in the semiconductor companies, too.

It doesn't take a genius to see that we are roughly on the right-hand edge of the S-curve graph above with many AI semiconductor names.

That said, maybe the valuation can go higher in the short term, but it looks like we are hitting a bit of a sentiment threshold here. In the base and bull cases, we are in the period of diminishing returns of varying magnitudes, and in the bear case, we are approaching a digestion zone for hyperscaler capex. The latter of which may cause downside volatility temporarily, but the black line indicated above (representing the smoothed non-volatile trend) would likely just show a horizontal movement (the beginning of the next s-curve and the end of the last).

The AI era we have just entered macroeconomically is probably endless by necessity and via design. Higher productivity and margin uplift will keep demand for AI semis robust. The logical counter is digestion, not collapse.

There Is No Final Peak, Just Endless Waves

The first s-curve was training-capacity scarcity, and the next curves include inference, agentic workflows, sovereign AI, enterprise automation, robotics, and physical AI. As outlined above, Nvidia is probably on the right-hand edge of the first AI semiconductor s-curve, not the final one. A flattening in one deployment wave can look like a peak, but it may just be the horizontal transition before the next compute-demand curve begins.

The latest reported fundamentals (full-year revenue up 65%; net income of $120.1B) and the consensus forward long-term EPS CAGR (of 38%) don't show a "peak" equity. It might be the peak of multiple expansion, but not of equity price growth. Instead, it's more like the end of the explosive re-rate and the start of the s-curve steady compounding phases.

For Q1 FY2027, Nvidia's guide is ~$78B of revenue, explicitly assuming no China Data Center compute revenue. Gross margin is anticipated to be around 75%. That is not what a "peak" guide looks like.

Microsoft's (MSFT) latest quarter showed $37.5B of capex, with about two-thirds of that being spent primarily on GPUs and CPUs, which are short-lived assets (a key aspect of the long-term AI semiconductor growth horizon). Microsoft has said that customer demand still exceeds supply.

The primary risk with this monster capex is concentration risk, as such strong capex is primarily seen among the main hyperscaler Big Tech companies (MSFT is used here as the simple case study). That said, we should remember that Big Tech hyperscalers are essentially acting as compute funnels with one large input and many small outputs to diversified customers. AI is heavily proliferating across almost every vector of every industry. Just ask around.

Autonomous Economies Rise As NVDA Grows

The IMF has estimated that about 60% of advanced-economy jobs are AI-exposed and that about half could be negatively affected and the other half complemented. According to a gov.uk report, the UK is even more exposed at around 70% due to its service-heavy economy.

That noted, agentic AI is unlikely to result in fast mass firings. Instead, we're looking at hiring restraint, with companies likely cutting contractors, slowing graduate hiring, compressing support functions, reducing junior analytical headcount, and automating back-office workflows. The effect of this on companies is margin expansion, but delayed unemployment affects. This should largely create relative macroeconomic stability during the transition toward fully autonomous workflows, though it will not be without dips or volatility in broad-based consumer demand. Importantly, according to McKinsey, only 39% of respondents in its survey reported enterprise-level EBIT impact from AI so far.

The trend here is simple and very meaningful for NVDA shareholders: AI converts labor cost into compute demand. If AI agents, as I and many others expect, replace clerical, support, coding, research, compliance, and analytical labor, then corporate opex will migrate toward cloud, GPUs, memory, networking, storage, and model-serving. Obviously, this is very bullish for Nvidia's long-run demand pool.

I admit there is the risk here that the transition is not managed well, and workforce dynamics should and must be dealt with respectfully and with care, because advanced economies (and particularly the rich) may capture more AI capital returns, while BPO-heavy emerging markets may face export-service pressure. This mimics the industrial revolution and needs to be analyzed by policymakers and company strategists (from a long-term demand-stability standpoint, based on macroeconomic interconnection).

Britain's Industrial Revolution: from 1780 to 1840, output per worker rose 46%, while real wages rose only 12%, while from 1840 to 1900, output rose 90% and real wages 123%, turning wage disruption into broad abundance. Hopefully we can make AI a smoother ride from the outset.

Price Target

Nvidia doesn't look too expensive to me here, but it's not cheap after its recent post-Iran-conflict dip. My 12-month price targets are $250 per share in the bull case, $225 in the base case, and $200 in the bear case. This is based on simple sentiment analysis done on-chart and not overcomplicated with any more depth. Company financials are embedded in my price targets as reflected by sentiment derived from operational and macro analysis. I do not consider a cyclical crash as a likely near-term, or even medium-term, outcome.

Can Custom Silicon Crash NVDA? No.

Bear analysts have stated that custom silicon could impair NVDA's long-term bull thesis. Let's examine this. Amazon (AMZN) says Trainium and Graviton now have a combined annual run rate of about $10B, and Trainium2 is fully subscribed, with Trainium3 supply nearly committed by mid-2026. That's a clear example of the friction being outlined by bears.

But custom ASICs don't kill Nvidia; they just cap the monopoly premium. It's a moderate erosion to the market share, but it's not really a structural break at all, especially as the TAM is expanding. Excuse the profundity, but could the TAM expand infinitely? Intergalactically over millennia? As long as we don't blow ourselves up, intelligent compute could evolve forever, just like humans. Semiconductors will be at the core, and we are just at the dawn of AI's existence.

Hyperscalers will use ASICs for predictable, high-volume inference where cost matters most. But Nvidia will most likely remain dominant in frontier training, high-performance inference, rack-scale systems, networking, software, and workloads needing flexibility.

I would call the primary risk "capex digestion," not "AI demand collapse." Nvidia is not immune to cycles, but the cycle is moving from training to agentic inference, not from boom to bust.

Conclusion: Buy

The stock is up 21% already from my prior Buy rating. I'm a value trader and investor at heart, even if I trade on sentiment perceptions. If I owned Nvidia stock, I'd see no reason to sell here. It's not the ideal point to go "all-in" as the sentiment-based valuation is a little overextended, but I still see 20% 12-month upside in the bull case, and the long-term trajectory is deserving of a Buy. I see a collapse as a tail risk, so holding through the enduring growth horizon is not only intelligent to my mind, it is wise.

AI demand is probably endless by economic necessity, but semiconductor revenue will come in s-curve waves. It will look like build, digest, optimize, and rebuild. That means Nvidia may face volatility around capex digestion, but a durable upward trend will persist as labor, software, and industrial processes are progressively converted into compute.
